message:
fix: edge_betweenness_subset (unhash) + find_negative_cycle (unhash) + triad_type exception class (br-r37-c1-39gx2)

Ninth follow-up to the unhashable-node series.  Probe after tm1tq
surfaced three more drifts:

  edge_betweenness_centrality_subset(sources=...)  fnx: <silent ok>    nx: TypeError
  find_negative_cycle(source=unhashable)           fnx: NetworkXError  nx: TypeError
  triad_type(G with order != 3)                    fnx: NetworkXError  nx: NetworkXAlgorithmError

The first two are unhashable-input drifts (one under-strict, one
wrong class).  The third is an exception-class refinement: nx uses
the more specific ``NetworkXAlgorithmError`` for triad-size
violations so callers can distinguish algorithmic invariants from
generic usage errors — fnx had been raising the parent
``NetworkXError`` instead.

Fix
---
``python/franken_networkx/__init__.py``:

- ``edge_betweenness_centrality_subset``: materialize sources/
  targets as lists and ``hash()``-validate every member up front
  (mirrors the fix in ``betweenness_centrality_subset`` from
  br-r37-c1-tm1tq).

- ``find_negative_cycle``: add ``hash(source)`` at the top of the
  wrapper.  nx delegates to bellman_ford which raises TypeError
  on unhashable; the Rust ``_raw_find_negative_cycle`` instead
  silently treats unhashable as 'no negative cycle' and raises
  ``NetworkXError("No negative cycle found.")``.

- ``triad_type``: replace ``NetworkXError`` with
  ``NetworkXAlgorithmError("G is not a triad (order-3 DiGraph)")``
  for the order-3 check, matching nx's exact wording and class.
  (The undirected guard from br-r37-c1-n7rgh still fires first.)

Tests
-----
New ``tests/python/test_edge_subset_negcycle_triad_parity.py``:
- 2 × edge_betweenness_subset unhashable sources/targets × 3 types
- 2 × find_negative_cycle unhashable × 3 (undirected + directed paths)
- 2 × triad_type wrong-size {1, 4} → NetworkXAlgorithmError
- 1 × triad_type undirected → NetworkXNotImplemented (regression for
  decorator-order: not_implemented_for fires before order-3 check)
- 4 × hashable-input regression guards (numeric correctness +
  exception-hierarchy assertion)

Full sweep
----------
7044 passed, 0 failed (was 7026 before — +19 new regression cases).

Note: ``minimum_cycle_basis_port_fixture_matrix_matches_networkx
[two-triangles-sharing-node]`` flaked once during full-sweep but
passes in isolation; pre-existing flakiness, not regression.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -13,6 +13,7 @@
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-2dwen","title":"tree_broadcast_center / tree_broadcast_time / maximal_matching missing multigraph guard (Rust-direct exports)","description":"Three Rust-direct re-exports lack the @not_implemented_for('multigraph') guard that nx applies. Scoped out of br-r37-c1-tqimg because they don't have Python wrappers — they're imported directly from the Rust extension.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.MultiGraph([(1,2),(2,3)])
  >>> fnx.tree_broadcast_center(g)        # silently runs Rust impl
  >>> nx.tree_broadcast_center(nx.MultiGraph([(1,2),(2,3)]))
  NetworkXNotImplemented: not implemented for multigraph type

  Same for tree_broadcast_time and maximal_matching.

For tree_broadcast_*, the directed guard is already correctly enforced by the Rust binding (both fnx and nx raise on DiGraph). Only the multigraph guard is missing.

Fix: replace the bare from franken_networkx._fnx import maximal_matching (and the tree_broadcast pair) with thin Python wrappers in __init__.py that add the multigraph guard before delegating to the Rust impl.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-27T08:27:05.832183564Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-27T08:35:51.919850945Z","closed_at":"2026-04-27T08:35:51.917253987Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-2hrfs","title":"find_cycle (undirected) edge-tuple direction drifts from nx","description":"fnx.find_cycle on an undirected graph yields edges in algorithm-canonical orientation, but nx yields edges in DFS-traversal direction (which depends on adj-iteration order).

Repro:
  edges = [('c','d'), ('a','b'), ('b','c'), ('d','e'), ('a','c')]
  fnx.find_cycle(g) -> [('c','a'), ('a','b'), ('b','c')]
  nx.find_cycle(gx) -> [('c','b'), ('b','a'), ('a','c')]

Edge tuples are reversed and the cycle is traversed in the opposite DFS direction. Fix: delegate find_cycle to nx for undirected graphs (the directed case already matches).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T12:59:04.011873621Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T13:08:43.076611064Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T13:08:43.074643528Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-2vdtt","title":"strongly_connected_components order disagrees with nx on multi-component DiGraphs","description":"fnx.strongly_connected_components currently materialises Rust's SCC output and applies reversed() to match nx. The reversed() flip happens to match small-symmetric cases but is wrong on inputs where Rust and nx disagree on component-discovery start node.

Repro:
  edges = [('c','d'),('a','b'),('b','c'),('d','a'),('c','a'),('e','f'),('f','e')]
  fnx -> [{'f','e'}, {'a','b','c','d'}]
  nx  -> [{'a','b','c','d'}, {'f','e'}]
  edges = [('a','b'),('b','a'),('c','d'),('d','c')]
  fnx -> [{'c','d'}, {'b','a'}]
  nx  -> [{'b','a'}, {'c','d'}]

Fix: delegate strongly_connected_components to nx for parity (Rust + reversed() heuristic is unsound).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T12:59:39.307393335Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T13:02:29.392395805Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T13:02:29.390417839Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"br-r37-c1-39gx2","title":"Parity drifts: edge_betweenness_centrality_subset, find_negative_cycle, triad_type","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-27T11:22:13.011850120Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-27T11:23:03.619717964Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-3dxfn","title":"parity: all_pairs_dijkstra_path / all_pairs_dijkstra_path_length outer-key iteration order doesn't match nx","description":"fnx.all_pairs_dijkstra_path and fnx.all_pairs_dijkstra_path_length yield outer-key (source) iteration in arbitrary Rust internal order (e.g. ['a','e','c','d','b']) instead of nx's node-insertion order (['c','d','a','b','e']). Drop-in code that iterates 'for source, dists in result.items(): ...' breaks. Same iteration-order family fixed for triangles/core_number/etc. Note: outer keys for all_pairs_shortest_path[_length] already match nx; only dijkstra variants need the fix.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T10:35:36.535013167Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T10:38:03.955576532Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T10:38:03.953597595Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit 40d5417. all_pairs_dijkstra_path / _length outer keys now iterate in node-insertion order matching nx. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-3lqi6","title":"DiGraph.reverse() / fnx.reverse() edge + adj iteration order drifts from nx","description":"fnx.reverse() (and DiGraph.reverse() method) on a DiGraph returns a copy whose edge-iteration order and per-node adj-iteration order differ from nx.

Repro:
  edges = [('a','b'),('b','c'),('c','d')]  # path
  fnx.reverse(dg).edges() -> [('c','b'),('d','c'),('b','a')]
  nx.reverse(dgx).edges() -> [('b','a'),('c','b'),('d','c')]

  edges = [(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,0)]  # cycle
  fnx -> [(1,0),(3,2),(0,3),(2,1)]
  nx  -> [(0,3),(1,0),(2,1),(3,2)]

  edges = [('c','d'),('a','b'),('b','c'),('d','a'),('c','a')]
  fnx adj['a'] = ['d', 'c']
  nx adj['a']  = ['c', 'd']

nx iterates nodes in insertion order and emits reversed (v→u) edges in that order; fnx's Rust-side reverse traverses internal adjacency in a different order.

The copy=False path matches. The copy=True path (default for both function and method) drifts.

Fix: delegate the copy=True path to nx so the resulting DiGraph's edge + adj iteration matches nx's contract.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T13:56:39.907591704Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T13:59:41.487970050Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T13:59:41.485884732Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-3qopf","title":"k_truss subgraph node order drifts from nx","description":"fnx.k_truss builds the resulting subgraph from Rust output which iterates nodes in canonical/sort order, while nx iterates them in input-graph node order.

Repro:
  edges = [('c','d'),('a','b'),('b','c'),('d','e'),('a','c')]
  fnx.k_truss(g, 2).nodes() -> ['a','b','c','d','e']
  nx.k_truss(gx, 2).nodes()  -> ['c','d','a','b','e']

Drop-in code that iterated R.nodes() / R.edges() / R.adj of the k-truss subgraph in nx's input-graph-order broke. Fix: delegate k_truss to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity + _from_nx_graph for output rehydration.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T16:37:05.403750981Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T16:39:25.048950577Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T16:39:25.046943262Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

@@ -9640,7 +9640,14 @@ def find_negative_cycle(G, source, weight="weight"):
     both directed and undirected negative-cycle discovery — by routing
     directed inputs through networkx's implementation while keeping
     undirected inputs on the native path.
+
+    br-r37-c1-39gx2: hash-validate ``source`` up front for nx-shaped
+    TypeError parity.  nx delegates to bellman_ford which raises
+    TypeError on unhashable; the Rust ``_raw_find_negative_cycle``
+    instead silently treats unhashable as 'no negative cycle' and
+    raises NetworkXError.
     """
+    hash(source)
     if G.is_directed():
         # Route directed graphs through upstream via the string-name
         # indirection so ``find_negative_cycle`` stays classified as
@@ -19368,7 +19375,11 @@ def triad_type(G):
         raise NetworkXNotImplemented("not implemented for undirected type")
     nodes = list(G.nodes())
     if len(nodes) != 3:
-        raise NetworkXError("triad_type requires exactly 3 nodes")
+        # br-r37-c1-39gx2: nx raises ``NetworkXAlgorithmError`` (not
+        # NetworkXError) with a specific 'G is not a triad (order-3
+        # DiGraph)' message — distinct exception class lets users
+        # catch algorithmic invariants separately from generic errors.
+        raise NetworkXAlgorithmError("G is not a triad (order-3 DiGraph)")
     return _fnx.triad_type_rust(G, nodes[0], nodes[1], nodes[2])
 
 
@@ -21231,9 +21242,20 @@ def edge_betweenness_centrality_subset(
         backend_kwargs,
     )
 
+    # br-r37-c1-39gx2: nx hashes each source/target into a dict —
+    # raises TypeError on the first unhashable element.  Without
+    # this check fnx silently ignores unhashable members.  Mirror
+    # the fix already in betweenness_centrality_subset (br-r37-c1-tm1tq).
+    sources = list(sources)
+    targets = list(targets)
+    for s in sources:
+        hash(s)
+    for t in targets:
+        hash(t)
+
     if weight is None and not G.is_multigraph():
         rust_scores = _edge_betweenness_centrality_subset_rust(
-            G, list(sources), list(targets), normalized
+            G, sources, targets, normalized
         )
         betweenness = dict.fromkeys(G.edges(), 0.0)
         is_directed = G.is_directed()

@@ -0,0 +1,179 @@
+"""Parity for edge_betweenness_centrality_subset (unhashable),
+find_negative_cycle (unhashable), and triad_type (wrong-size graph
+exception class).
+
+Bead br-r37-c1-39gx2 (ninth follow-up to the unhashable-node series
+plus a triad_type exception-class drift).
+
+Three drifts found in the post-tm1tq probe:
+
+  edge_betweenness_centrality_subset(sources=[unhashable])  fnx: <silent ok>      nx: TypeError
+  find_negative_cycle(source=unhashable)                    fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError
+  triad_type(G with order != 3)                             fnx: NetworkXError    nx: NetworkXAlgorithmError
+
+``edge_betweenness_centrality_subset`` was UNDER-strict (silent on
+unhashable members) — same fix as ``betweenness_centrality_subset``.
+``find_negative_cycle`` raised the wrong class (its Rust impl
+silently returns 'no cycle' on unhashable, then NetworkXError fires);
+nx delegates to bellman_ford which raises TypeError.  ``triad_type``
+used the generic ``NetworkXError`` class for size-violations;
+nx uses the more specific ``NetworkXAlgorithmError`` so callers can
+distinguish algorithm-invariant breaches from misc usage errors.
+"""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import pytest
+
+import franken_networkx as fnx
+
+try:
+    import networkx as nx
+    HAS_NX = True
+except ImportError:
+    HAS_NX = False
+
+needs_nx = pytest.mark.skipif(not HAS_NX, reason="networkx not installed")
+
+UNHASHABLE = [
+    pytest.param([1, 2], id="list"),
+    pytest.param({1, 2}, id="set"),
+    pytest.param({"a": 1}, id="dict"),
+]
+
+
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# edge_betweenness_centrality_subset — TypeError on unhashable sources/targets
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_edge_betweenness_subset_unhashable_sources_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        fnx.edge_betweenness_centrality_subset(G, [val], [1])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        nx.edge_betweenness_centrality_subset(GX, [val], [1])
+
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_edge_betweenness_subset_unhashable_targets_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        fnx.edge_betweenness_centrality_subset(G, [1], [val])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        nx.edge_betweenness_centrality_subset(GX, [1], [val])
+
+
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# find_negative_cycle — TypeError on unhashable source
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_find_negative_cycle_unhashable_source_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        fnx.find_negative_cycle(G, val)
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        nx.find_negative_cycle(GX, val)
+
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_find_negative_cycle_unhashable_source_typeerror_directed(val):
+    """Directed path delegates to nx — verify TypeError parity there too."""
+    G = fnx.DiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    GX = nx.DiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        fnx.find_negative_cycle(G, val)
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        nx.find_negative_cycle(GX, val)
+
+
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# triad_type — NetworkXAlgorithmError on order != 3
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_triad_type_too_few_nodes_raises_algorithm_error():
+    G = fnx.DiGraph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.DiGraph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(fnx.NetworkXAlgorithmError, match=r"order-3"):
+        fnx.triad_type(G)
+    with pytest.raises(nx.NetworkXAlgorithmError, match=r"order-3"):
+        nx.triad_type(GX)
+
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_triad_type_too_many_nodes_raises_algorithm_error():
+    G = fnx.DiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 4)])
+    GX = nx.DiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 4)])
+    with pytest.raises(fnx.NetworkXAlgorithmError, match=r"order-3"):
+        fnx.triad_type(G)
+    with pytest.raises(nx.NetworkXAlgorithmError, match=r"order-3"):
+        nx.triad_type(GX)
+
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_triad_type_undirected_still_raises_not_implemented():
+    """Undirected guard (br-r37-c1-n7rgh) takes precedence over the
+    order-3 check — both nx and fnx fire the @not_implemented_for
+    decorator first."""
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 1)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 1)])
+    with pytest.raises(fnx.NetworkXNotImplemented):
+        fnx.triad_type(G)
+    with pytest.raises(nx.NetworkXNotImplemented):
+        nx.triad_type(GX)
+
+
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# Regressions — hashable inputs unaffected
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_edge_betweenness_subset_hashable_unchanged():
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 4)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 4)])
+    f = fnx.edge_betweenness_centrality_subset(G, [1], [4])
+    n = nx.edge_betweenness_centrality_subset(GX, [1], [4])
+    f_norm = {tuple(sorted(k)): round(v, 9) for k, v in f.items()}
+    n_norm = {tuple(sorted(k)): round(v, 9) for k, v in n.items()}
+    assert f_norm == n_norm
+
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_find_negative_cycle_hashable_unchanged():
+    """Find a real negative cycle on a hashable directed graph."""
+    G = fnx.DiGraph()
+    G.add_weighted_edges_from([(0, 1, 1), (1, 2, -3), (2, 0, 1)])
+    GX = nx.DiGraph()
+    GX.add_weighted_edges_from([(0, 1, 1), (1, 2, -3), (2, 0, 1)])
+    f = fnx.find_negative_cycle(G, 0)
+    n = nx.find_negative_cycle(GX, 0)
+    # Order-of-emission may vary; compare as a cycle set.
+    assert set(f) == set(n)
+
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_triad_type_3node_unchanged():
+    G = fnx.DiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 1)])
+    GX = nx.DiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 1)])
+    assert fnx.triad_type(G) == nx.triad_type(GX)
+
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_triad_type_algorithm_error_inherits_from_networkx_exception():
+    """NetworkXAlgorithmError descends from NetworkXException (not
+    NetworkXError) — both fnx and nx agree on this hierarchy.  Code
+    catching NetworkXException still works after the class refinement."""
+    G = fnx.DiGraph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(fnx.NetworkXException):
+        fnx.triad_type(G)
+    assert issubclass(fnx.NetworkXAlgorithmError, fnx.NetworkXException)
+    assert not issubclass(fnx.NetworkXAlgorithmError, fnx.NetworkXError)
